ancestors.notes: Direct descent from Old/Medieval Castilian (Toledo norm), itself from Hispanic Vulgar Latin.
Basque/Aquitanian substrate in the Castilian homeland: the Latin f->h change (filium>hijo), reinforcement of the 5-vowel system.
Visigothic (Gothic) superstrate: lexical layer (guerra, guardar, ropa, ganso) and some onomastics.
Andalusi Arabic adstrate: ~4000 loanwords (alcohol, almohada, aceite), inherited via the medieval stage.

name: Castilian Spanish
notes: Standard Peninsular Castilian Spanish (español castellano) per the Real Academia Española (RAE) and Asociación de Academias de la Lengua Española. Spoken natively by ~47 million in Spain; ~500 million worldwide as a first or second language. KEY FEATURES of Standard Castilian (distinción variety): (1) DISTINCIÓN: /θ/ ≠ /s/ — ⟨c⟩ before e/i and ⟨z⟩ = /θ/ (interdental fricative); absent in Latin American Spanish and in Andalusian/Canarian (seseo). (2) APICO-ALVEOLAR /s/ [s̺]: the standard Castilian s is produced with the tongue tip (apex) touching the upper alveolar ridge — a conservative feature shared with Asturian and Galician but absent from Portuguese (predorsal). (3) YEÍSMO: ⟨ll⟩ = ⟨y⟩ = /ʝ/ in modern standard (RAE 2010 norm); the historical /ʎ/ distinction is preserved in some regions (NW Spain, Río de la Plata). (4) BETACISM: ⟨b⟩ = ⟨v⟩ = /b/ (complete, unlike Portuguese). (5) VELAR FRICATIVE /x/: ⟨j⟩ and ⟨g+e,i⟩ = /x/ (or [χ] before back vowels in some varieties). (6) SILENT ⟨h⟩: etymological F- underwent F→H→∅ change: FILIU→hijo, FACERE→hacer. (7) INTERVOCALIC LENITION: voiced stops /b, d, ɡ/ → spirants [β, ð, ɣ] between vowels. (8) Word-final /d/ → [ð] or [∅]: verdad [berˈdað]/[berˈda]. Sources: Quilis (1999), Penny (2002), Hualde (2014).

sources.author: Penny, R.
sources.title: A History of the Spanish Language

stress.notes: RAE accentuation rules: written accent wins (á é í ó ú). Default paroxytone for words ending in a vowel, -n, or -s. Words ending in any other consonant (r, l, d, z, j, x, k, f, and consonant clusters) are oxytone. The final_stress_endings list encodes the oxytone-attracting consonant endings; penult_stress_endings encodes -n/-s/-ns as explicit paroxytone attractors (overriding the default for coda -n/-s when stress position is ambiguous). Proparoxytones (esdrújulas) always carry a written accent so marked_vowels captures them. CLITICS: cliticless_words lists the Spanish prosodic clitics (palabras atonas) — definite articles, atonic indefinite article, al/del contractions, monosyllabic prepositions plus para, the atonic conjunctions y/e/o/u/ni/que and conditional si, the weak object pronouns me/te/se/le/les/nos/os and the prenominal possessives mi/tu/su(+plurals) — which lean on their host and carry no word stress (Navarro Tomas 1977, Manual de pronunciacion espanola, palabras inacentuadas; Hualde 2005, The Sounds of Spanish, prosody; RAE-ASALE Nueva gramatica, prosodia). Tonic homographs written with an accent (si 'yes', el 'he', mi/tu 'me/you', se 'I know') and the negation 'no' are excluded.

location.notes: Madrid — the centre of the Castilian norm. Glottolog's point for cast1244 is the generic Spanish point and says nothing about Castilian specifically. [Wikidata Q2807 P625]
